deberta_base_nli.description: A stronger DeBERTa NLI model that reads each post-comment pair and scores whether the comment agrees with or disagrees with the post.

deberta_small_nli.description: A smaller DeBERTa NLI model using the same agree/disagree framing. It is lighter and often more decisive, which creates useful contrast with the larger model.

>But, of course it’s an admission of the fact that their products are harmful. They are lobbying against a social media ban for kids, but at the same time are banning their own kids from using it. That’s because they know how harmful their algorithms are.

Who in the article are you referring to? The social media CEO mentioned says he would let his kids use it if they lived in the U.S.

>And over all they do have a lot to answer for, that’s just a fact

My comment literally started by saying "Out of all the bad things they have done". Nowhere in my comment did I say they have nothing to answer for, you are arguing against your imaginary version of me.

There's another major reason that billionaires don't want their children on social media that reporters neglect. The kids are massive targets for hackers and other malicious groups. Silicon Valley is also filled to the bring with pseudoscience based wellness trends and other crap, so I would take everything they do with a grain of salt.

> In the past year, Australia and Malaysia became the first countries to ban adolescents under 16 from using social media. And several other countries, including France, Denmark, and the United Kingdom, are considering similar legislation.

Why include this while having zero mention of how these bans are undermining and destroying privacy online? It wasn't that long ago that everyone was going after tech companies for violating people's privacy, and reporters seem to magically forgot that. Now they seem to championing making tech companies funnel personal information into the hands of different tech companies, as though privacy is the enemy now.

and that is the most dangerous part. Too many people are willing to surrender their privacy iand believed the government’s trojan horse of “won’t anyone think of the children”.  There are better ways to do this such as educating children on internet safety and usage, and I don’t believe social media ban would be effective at all.

opposing_side.summary: Key arguments on this side focus on media, social, agree, social media.
representative_comments:
Calling it now that this is nothing to do with protecting children and it's all about image and a foot in the door to controlling everyone online.  


It always begins with "think of the children" but it's an excuse.  


I do agree however social media is dumb for anyone under about 16. But the government trying to control it... Even more dumb

Good. I would usually be on the side of thinking the government will take this too far and become too restrictive, but social media is god awful even for adults. I can’t imagine what it’s doing to developing brains. Protect the kids!

Social media these days does seem more volatile than I remember it being, I’m not sure if that’s due to an increase in bots and agitator types who exist solely to create conflict (see all the accounts from India and other places on Twitter for example), or due to a generation of boys being raised by the kinds of people who worship Andrew Tate manosphere types and are Trump voters. 

But it’s a parent’s job to really make sure they are aware of what their children are doing online and doing their best to educate them on how they should be acting, and making them aware on how to handle shit like this, as horrible as it can be. 

I do understand and even would support some sort of social media ban for younger teenagers. The problem is that nearly every single idea that touches on this subject from lawmakers ends up leading to some sort of government surveillance and or censorship bill that strips us completely of our internet anonymity, which everybody should disagree with 100%.

Years of goodwill built up over decades, gone. This time last year I was raving to my girlfriend about how happy I was to be back on Windows after being stuck on a Mac laptop for years. Well, so much for that. Fuck these people.
Especially if Windows 12 is what they claim. A subscription based, AI slop generator is a big no from me.

Don't you think chasing the 'mass market' is what got us in this mess in the first place? Why should any one company have that much power of people's computing? That's just setting up another for another Microsoft or Apple.

opposing_side.summary: Key arguments on this side focus on windows, linux, agree, yes.
representative_comments: Yep. Been considering it for years but laziness and fear of learning a new OS has prevented me. Thanks for lighting a fire under my ass Microsoft, probably would have continued using your shit product for decades otherwise.

> Some parts of the OS might also be locked away behind a subscription model that are expected to be “advanced AI services”, etc.

I see this as a win. They might actually keep the slop behind a paywall which nobody is interested in. Overall a lighter experience.

Yeah same.    It’s one thing if people feel the they shouldn’t have to disable all that stuff.   My first thought when I got my test key from Microsoft was that setup has gotten more over the top.  With that said, there were plenty of resources available even back in 2022 with easy steps on how to setup windows to be AI and data collection free.  It seems like people would just rather complain than take the bull by the horns.

Good. Microsoft needs to fall on it's face and HARD. Maybe they'll do a Windows without all the bloat, AI, forced installs of useless software and spyware.

They've spent the last 20 years figuring out how they can harvest more data to sell from us with each update! What the hell did they think was going to happen??? Once I heard them talk about 'phasing out' the keyboard in favor of telling the computer what you wanted it to do via it's stupid ass 'copilot' I immediately started looking into linux compatibility for my everyday driver programs. You are absolutely correct. The pushback I get when I tell people to dump Amazon Alexa Google Facebook Twitter etc is just insane. We are being pushed more and more to do everything online under guise of advancement. No. No. No. I dont want to give you all my info just to park my fucking car. Or turn on my ac. I wholeheartedly feel the same. What happened to the push for net neutrality & respecting peoples online privacy? It's like the world is trying to go in the exact opposite direction of all that now. This will only lead to putting people's IDs at risk of hackers & will do very little to actually stop mental health issues social media causes for some people which can affect people of all ages not just the 16 or under crowd.

At this point this has just become a smokescreen that tech companies use to hide their much more nefarious businesses in surveillance, war, and grooming children to become loyal gullible consumers.

YouTube isn’t worth a shit anymore, anyway… 

Haven’t visited that site in years. 

Fuck Alphabet, Meta, Amazon, Nvidia, Microslop, et. al…

I wonder how they’ll continue to make profits when the AI circle jerk has eliminated the jobs of the majority of tax playing humans… Where do these fart sniffing oligarchs see this trend going?

I wonder if robots like endless bullshit ads… will they even watch YouTube?

The internet is about to get much worse… human generated content is being gobbled up by AI and spit out without the creators getting clicks and revenue. People just should pay for YT premium or let the ads run before everything is just AI slop.

opposing_side.summary: Key arguments on this side focus on agree, yes, good, don.
representative_comments:
I agree with that 100%.  It's also a problem that they basically have a monopoly on this kind of service.

I'm already doing what I can to mitigate my reliance on streaming platforms or any online platform/software at all.  I lived before they existed and I can live afterwards as well.

I agree about enshittification.  We're on the same page there.

Yes, it's in response, but they had to invest capital to build initially, and spend capital to maintain it.  Hard drives die all the time, network infrastructure needs to be replaced, fiber lines get cut accidentally, there's a million things that cost money to run a service like YouTube.  Things that cost even if ZERO people watched.

It won't surprise me if they stop allowing 4k for free users, or not allowing uploads over a certain size below a certain number of subscribers.  We all have to meet in the middle.
I honestly think it's a good thing. The faster the enshitify the platform, the faster we get viable alternatives that are fair with their context creators in their revenue split.
